__data__.text:
Problem: A logistics center must devise a procurement and storage strategy for a critical fuel additive over a six-week operational window.
The initial stockpile at the commencement of this window is 37 thousand liters.
In each week \( t = 1, \dots, 6 \), the distributor permits the center to submit a requisition whose volume must fall between 0 and 28 thousand liters.
Nevertheless, every requisition submitted will require a lead time of 1 week before it becomes available to fulfill consumption requirements or augment the stockpile.
The consumption of the additive in each week is specified as follows:
* Week 1: consumption 33 thousand liters
* Week 2: consumption 44 thousand liters
* Week 3: consumption 21 thousand liters
* Week 4: consumption 23 thousand liters
* Week 5: consumption 34 thousand liters
* Week 6: consumption 47 thousand liters

Stockouts are tolerated, but any unsatisfied consumption is entirely forfeited and will not be deferred to a subsequent week.
Throughout the planning window, additive quantities may be treated as continuous, and the aggregate volume of physical stock held at any moment must not surpass the tank farm capacity of 48 thousand liters.

In week 6, the maximum permissible requisition volume is 2 thousand liters.
In week 3, the physical stock on hand must be no less than 39 thousand liters.

The aggregate expenditure over the planning window comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 3 per thousand liters requisitioned; the storage cost, amounting to 2 per thousand liters of stock transferred from one week to the next; and the penalty for unmet consumption, amounting to 11 per thousand liters of unsatisfied demand. The objective is to ascertain the optimal requisition volume for each week and to monitor the consequent stockpile and shortfall levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

Problem: A logistics firm must devise a procurement and storage schedule for a critical component over a 6‑week operational window.  
The starting stock at the opening of the planning interval is 90 units.  
In each week \( t = 1, \dots, 6 \), the vendor permits the firm to submit a procurement request whose volume must be between 1 and 30 units.  
Nevertheless, every requested shipment requires a lead time of 3 weeks before it becomes available to meet requirements or replenish the stockpile.  
The weekly requirement for the component is specified as follows:  
* Week 1: requirement 35 units  
* Week 2: requirement 54 units  
* Week 3: requirement 46 units  
* Week 4: requirement 36 units  
* Week 5: requirement 59 units  
* Week 6: requirement 52 units  

Stock‑outs are tolerated, but any unsatisfied requirement is lost and will not be carried forward.  
Throughout the planning window, component quantities may be treated as continuous values, and the aggregate quantity physically present in the warehouse at any moment must not surpass the storage limit of 90 units.  

In week 4, the maximum allowable procurement volume is 30 units.  
In week 5, the on‑hand stock must be no less than 77 units.  

The aggregate expenditure over the planning horizon comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 1 per unit requested; the storage cost, amounting to 2 per unit held over from one week to the next; and the penalty for unmet demand, amounting to 8 per unit of unsatisfied requirement.  
Determine the optimal procurement quantity for each week and monitor the consequent stock and shortage levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

__data__.text:
Problem: A retail outlet must devise a procurement and stock management strategy for a seasonal product over a sales window of 6 weeks.
The initial stock at the start of the window is 45 items.
In each week t = 1, ..., 6, the distributor permits the outlet to place a shipment whose size must be between 12 and 73 items.
However, each shipment placed will take 2 week(s) to be delivered before it can be used to meet sales or replenish stock.
The projected sales for the product in each week are as follows:
* Week 1: sales 53 items
* Week 2: sales 36 items
* Week 3: sales 46 items
* Week 4: sales 42 items
* Week 5: sales 29 items
* Week 6: sales 51 items

Stockouts are tolerated, but any unsatisfied sales will be lost and not carried forward.
Throughout the sales window, product quantities are permitted to be fractional, and the total amount of physical stock on the premises at any moment must not exceed the storage limit of 49 items.

In week 1, the maximum shipment size is 33 items.
By week 6, the physical stock on hand must be no less than 2 items.

The total expense over the sales window comprises three elements: the procurement expense, which equals 2 per item shipped; the storage expense, which equals 3 per item of stock held from one week to the next; and the lost‑sales penalty, which equals 6 per item of unmet sales. Please determine the optimal shipment size for each week and monitor the resulting stock and stockout levels so as to minimize the total expense.

Problem: A regional water utility must devise a pumping and reservoir management plan over a 6-week planning window.  
The initial water volume stored in the reservoir at the start of the planning period is 96 million gallons.  
In each week \( t = 1, \dots, 6 \), the utility may schedule a pumping run from a distant aquifer; the volume pumped must be between 0 and 72 million gallons.  
However, each pumping run requires a 4-week transit time through the pipeline network before the water becomes available for consumption or storage.  
The weekly consumption forecast (in million gallons) is as follows:  
* Week 1: 36  
* Week 2: 30  
* Week 3: 10  
* Week 4: 23  
* Week 5: 10  
* Week 6: 55  

Supply shortfalls are tolerated, but any unmet consumption is simply lost and not carried forward.  
Throughout the planning horizon, water volumes can be treated as continuous quantities, and the total volume stored in the reservoir at any time must not exceed its capacity of 96 million gallons.

In week 6, the maximum allowable pumping volume is restricted to 25 million gallons.  
In week 3, the on-site stored volume must be at least 35 million gallons.

The total cost over the planning horizon comprises three elements: the pumping cost, which is 5 per million gallons pumped; the storage cost, which is 1 per million gallons held from one week to the next; and the shortage penalty, which is 9 per million gallons of unmet consumption. The utility seeks to determine the optimal pumping volume for each week and to monitor the resulting reservoir storage and shortage levels so as to minimize the total cost.

Facility Delta: Positioned at grid reference (31,122)

__data__.text:
Problem: A distribution center must devise a procurement and stock management strategy for a critical component across a 7-week planning window.
The initial stock level at the start of the planning interval is 16 units.
In each week t = 1, ..., 7, the vendor permits the center to issue a procurement request whose volume must fall between 12 and 71 units.
Notably, each procurement request will require a lead time of 2 weeks before the component becomes available to fulfill requirements or augment stock.
The weekly requirement for the component is specified as follows:
* Week 1: requirement 51 units
* Week 2: requirement 17 units
* Week 3: requirement 53 units
* Week 4: requirement 55 units
* Week 5: requirement 49 units
* Week 6: requirement 28 units
* Week 7: requirement 60 units

Stockouts are tolerated, but any unsatisfied requirement is considered lost and will not be carried forward.
Throughout the planning window, component quantities may be treated as continuous, and the aggregate volume of physical stock held at any point must not surpass the storage limit of 54 units.

In the seventh week, the maximum allowable procurement volume is restricted to 24 units.
In the first week, the physical stock on hand must be no less than 50 units.

The aggregate expenditure over the planning horizon comprises three elements: the procurement cost, calculated at 6 per unit procured; the storage cost, calculated at 3 per unit of stock held over from one week to the next; and the penalty for unmet requirements, calculated at 7 per unit of lost requirement. The objective is to ascertain the optimal procurement volume for each week and to monitor the consequent stock and shortage levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

__data__.text:
Problem: A city's water utility must devise a pumping and reservoir management strategy for a critical water source across a weekly schedule of 7 days.  
The initial water level in the reservoir at the start of the planning week is 13 megaliters.  
On each day \( t = 1, \dots, 7 \), the pumping station can be activated to transfer an amount of water from a remote aquifer; the pumped volume on any given day must be between 19 and 51 megaliters.  
However, due to transmission delays, water pumped on a given day will only become available in the reservoir after 1 full day has passed.  
The daily municipal water demand for each day is as follows:  
* Day 1: demand 16 megaliters  
* Day 2: demand 22 megaliters  
* Day 3: demand 39 megaliters  
* Day 4: demand 34 megaliters  
* Day 5: demand 33 megaliters  
* Day 6: demand 44 megaliters  
* Day 7: demand 19 megaliters  

Temporary supply deficits are permissible, but any unmet daily demand is simply lost and cannot be recovered later.  
Throughout the week, water volumes are treated as continuous quantities, and the total volume stored in the reservoir at any moment must not exceed its capacity of 44 megaliters.  

On the fourth day, the maximum allowable pumping volume is restricted to 39 megaliters.  
By the end of the seventh day, the reservoir must contain at least 31 megaliters.  

The total operational cost over the week comprises three elements: the pumping cost, which is 5 per megaliter pumped; the storage cost, which is 2 per megaliter held overnight from one day to the next; and a penalty for unmet demand, assessed at 12 per megaliter of daily shortfall.  
The objective is to determine the optimal pumping volume for each day and to monitor the resulting reservoir levels and daily deficits so as to minimize the total cost.

pumping_cost = 5
storage_cost = 2
penalty_cost = 12

Problem: A regional air courier must dispatch a single aircraft to seven distinct facilities:
Facility Alpha: Positioned at grid reference (21,185)
Facility Beta: Positioned at grid reference (33,3)
Facility Gamma: Positioned at grid reference (102,173)
Facility Delta: Positioned at grid reference (106,80)
Facility Epsilon: Positioned at grid reference (0,54)
Facility Zeta: Positioned at grid reference (3,183)
Facility Eta: Positioned at grid reference (193,0)

The flight distances between facilities, measured in nautical miles, are predetermined:
Alpha to Gamma: 81.9 nm
Alpha to Delta: 135.1 nm
Alpha to Epsilon: 132.7 nm
Alpha to Zeta: 18.1 nm
Alpha to Eta: 252.6 nm
Beta to Gamma: 183.5 nm
Beta to Delta: 106.1 nm

Epsilon to Zeta: 129.0 nm

The objective is to determine the minimal total flight distance for a closed circuit that visits each facility precisely once before returning to the point of origin.

Problem: A hospital must devise a procurement and blood‑plasma storage strategy for a critical therapeutic agent over an 8‑week planning cycle.  
The initial stock at the start of the planning window is 92 liters.  
In each week \(t = 1,\dots,8\), the supplier permits the hospital to request a shipment whose volume must be between 8 and 25 liters.  
Every shipment, however, requires a lead time of 2 weeks before it becomes available for patient treatment or to replenish the storage.  
The weekly consumption of the agent is as follows:  
* Week 1: 60 liters  
* Week 2: 38 liters  
* Week 3: 37 liters  
* Week 4: 53 liters  
* Week 5: 36 liters  
* Week 6: 11 liters  
* Week 7: 41 liters  
* Week 8: 30 liters  

Supply shortfalls are tolerated, but any unsatisfied consumption is lost and cannot be deferred to later weeks.  
Throughout the 8‑week horizon, agent quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume kept in the refrigerated storage at any moment must not exceed the capacity limit of 92 liters.  

In week 6, the maximum allowable shipment volume is restricted to 19 liters.  
In week 3, the on‑hand stock must be maintained at a level of at least 1 liter.  

The overall cost comprises three elements: the procurement cost, charged at 1 per liter ordered; the storage cost, charged at 1 per liter carried over from one week to the next; and a shortage penalty, levied at 11 per liter of unmet consumption.  
The objective is to decide the optimal shipment volume for each week and to monitor the resulting stock and shortage levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

Problem: A regional water utility must devise a pumping and reservoir management schedule across an 8-week operational cycle.  
The initial water volume stored in the main reservoir at the start of the cycle is 83 megaliters.  
In each week \( t = 1,\dots,8 \), the utility may activate a pumping station to transfer water from a distant source; the pumped volume in any week must be between 1 and 48 megaliters.  
However, due to pipeline transit time, water pumped in a given week becomes available only after a 2‑week delay.  
Weekly consumer demand for water is projected as follows:  
* Week 1: 36 megaliters  
* Week 2: 23 megaliters  
* Week 3: 40 megaliters  
* Week 4: 41 megaliters  
* Week 5: 54 megaliters  
* Week 6: 42 megaliters  
* Week 7: 30 megaliters  
* Week 8: 41 megaliters  

Supply deficits are tolerated, but any unmet weekly demand is lost and cannot be recovered later.  
Water volumes are treated as continuous quantities, and the total volume stored in the reservoir at any moment must not exceed its capacity of 83 megaliters.

In week 4, the maximum allowable pumping volume is restricted to 2 megaliters.  
In week 6, the stored volume must be maintained at a minimum of 60 megaliters.

The total operational cost comprises three elements: the pumping cost, which is 2 per megaliter pumped; the storage cost, which is 3 per megaliter held from one week to the next; and a deficit penalty, which is 13 per megaliter of unmet weekly demand. Determine the optimal pumping volume for each week and the consequent reservoir storage and deficit levels so as to minimize the total cost.

Problem: A shipping company must devise a cargo procurement and storage schedule for a critical commodity across an 8-week voyage timeline.  
The initial stock available at the voyage’s commencement is 56 tons.  
In each week t = 1, …, 8, the supplier permits the company to arrange a shipment whose tonnage must fall between 3 and 67 tons.  
Nevertheless, every shipment arranged will require a 2-week transit delay before it becomes available for meeting consumption or replenishing stock.  
The required consumption of the commodity each week is as follows:  
* Week 1: 12 tons  
* Week 2: 10 tons  
* Week 3: 26 tons  
* Week 4: 35 tons  
* Week 5: 43 tons  
* Week 6: 47 tons  
* Week 7: 55 tons  
* Week 8: 35 tons  

Stockouts are tolerated, but any unsatisfied consumption will be permanently lost.  
Throughout the voyage, commodity amounts may be fractional, and the total quantity of physical stock on hand at any moment must not surpass the vessel’s storage limit of 56 tons.  

In week 1, the maximum shipment tonnage allowed is 12 tons.  
By week 5, the physical stock on hand must be no less than 19 tons.  

The aggregate expense over the voyage comprises three elements: the procurement expense, amounting to 3 per ton shipped; the storage expense, amounting to 2 per ton of stock retained from one week to the next; and the shortage penalty, amounting to 9 per ton of unmet consumption. Determine the optimal shipment tonnage for each week and monitor the resulting stock and shortage levels so as to minimize the total expense.

__data__.text:
Problem: A regional power grid must devise a procurement and storage schedule for natural gas over an upcoming 8-hour operational window.
The initial gas reserve in storage at the start of the window is 18 thousand cubic feet.
In each hour t = 1, ..., 8, the gas supplier permits the grid operator to request a delivery volume which must be between 13 and 77 thousand cubic feet.
However, each requested delivery undergoes a transmission delay of 4 hour(s) before it becomes available to meet consumption or augment reserves.
The projected gas consumption for each hour is as follows:
* Hour 1: consumption 59 thousand cubic feet
* Hour 2: consumption 42 thousand cubic feet
* Hour 3: consumption 54 thousand cubic feet
* Hour 4: consumption 43 thousand cubic feet
* Hour 5: consumption 36 thousand cubic feet
* Hour 6: consumption 42 thousand cubic feet
* Hour 7: consumption 29 thousand cubic feet
* Hour 8: consumption 17 thousand cubic feet

Supply shortfalls are tolerated, but any unfulfilled consumption is simply lost and not deferred to a later hour.
Throughout the planning window, gas volumes are treated as continuous quantities, and the total volume held in storage at any instant must not exceed the facility's capacity of 45 thousand cubic feet.

During hour 2, the maximum allowable delivery request is 66 thousand cubic feet.
During hour 3, the volume physically present in storage must be no less than 1 thousand cubic feet.

The aggregate operational expenditure comprises three elements: the procurement cost, calculated at 4 per thousand cubic feet ordered; the storage cost, calculated at 1 per thousand cubic feet kept in reserve from one hour to the next; and the penalty for shortfall, calculated at 7 per thousand cubic feet of unmet consumption. The objective is to ascertain the optimal delivery volume to request each hour and to monitor the consequent reserve and deficit levels in order to minimize the total expenditure.

__data__.text:
Problem: A regional water reservoir must devise a pumping and storage schedule over a 9-day forecast window.  
The initial water volume at the start of the planning interval is 81 megaliters.  
In each day \( t = 1, \dots, 9 \), the pumping station can extract from a nearby river an amount that must be between 8 and 37 megaliters.  
However, each pumping action requires a 1-day delay before the water becomes available for meeting consumption or augmenting the reservoir.  
The daily consumption forecast is as follows:  
* Day 1: 22 megaliters  
* Day 2: 55 megaliters  
* Day 3: 28 megaliters  
* Day 4: 59 megaliters  
* Day 5: 38 megaliters  
* Day 6: 42 megaliters  
* Day 7: 48 megaliters  
* Day 8: 39 megaliters  
* Day 9: 44 megaliters  

Water deficits are allowed, but any unmet consumption is lost and cannot be recovered later.  
Throughout the horizon, water quantities are treated as continuous, and the total stored volume at any moment must not exceed the reservoir capacity of 81 megaliters.

On day 2, the maximum pumping volume is limited to 13 megaliters.  
On day 7, the stored volume must be at least 31 megaliters.

The total operational cost comprises three parts: the pumping cost, which is 1 per megaliter extracted; the storage cost, which is 1 per megaliter kept overnight; and a penalty for water shortage, which is 15 per megaliter of unmet consumption. Determine the optimal pumping volume for each day and monitor the resulting storage and deficit levels so as to minimize the total cost.

max_pump = 37

min_storage_day7 = 31

storage_cost = 1
